user_input: What is the main problem addressed by the residual learning framework in deep neural networks?
retrieved_contexts:
['Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
Kaiming He Xiangyu Zhang Shaoqing Ren Jian Sun
Microsoft Research
fkahe, v-xiangz, v-shren, jiansun g@microsoft.com
Abstract
Deeper neural networks are more difﬁcult to train. We
present a residual learning framework to ease the training
of networks that are substantially deeper than those used
previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learn-
ing residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, in-
stead of learning unreferenced functi', 'ing residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, in-
stead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide com-
prehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual
networks are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from
considerably increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we
evaluate residual nets with a depth of up to 152 layers—8 \x02
deeper than VGG nets [41] but still having lower complex-
ity. An ensemble of these residual nets achieves 3.57% error
on the ImageNet testset. ', 'od or better than the constructed solution
(or unable to do so in feasible time).
In this paper, we address the degradation problem by
introducing a deep residual learning framework. In-
stead of hoping each few stacked layers directly ﬁt a
desired underlying mapping, we explicitly let these lay-
ers ﬁt a residual mapping. Formally, denoting the desired
underlying mapping as H(x), we let the stacked nonlinear
layers ﬁt another mapping of F(x) :=H(x)\x00x. The orig-
inal mapping is recast into F(x)+']
response:
The main problem addressed by the residual learning framework in deep neural networks is the degradation of performance, which can be introduced by a lack of optimization (or inability to do so within feasible time). In this paper, the authors introduce a deep residual learning framework that explicitly introduces residual functions as learn-
ing residual functions with reference to layer inputs instead of unreferenced functions. This framework is shown to be easier to optimize and achieve improved accuracy from considerably increased depths.

📄 Source: ResNet.pdf
reference: The degradation problem, where accuracy gets saturated and then degrades rapidly as the network depth increases.

retrieved_contexts:
['od or better than the constructed solution

In this paper, we address the degradation problem by
introducing a deep residual learning framework. In-
stead of hoping each few stacked layers directly ﬁt a
desired underlying mapping, we explicitly let these lay-
ers ﬁt a residual mapping. Formally, denoting the desired
underlying mapping as H(x), we let the stacked nonlinear
layers ﬁt another mapping of F(x) :=H(x)\x00x. The orig-
inal mapping is recast into F(x)+', 'ive the weights of the multiple nonlinear lay-
ers toward zero to approach identity mappings.
In real cases, it is unlikely that identity mappings are op-
timal, but our reformulation may help to precondition the
problem. If the optimal function is closer to an identity
mapping than to a zero mapping, it should be easier for the
solver to ﬁnd the perturbations with reference to an identity
mapping, than to learn the function as a new one. We show
by experiments (Fig. 7) that the learned residual', 'dence shows that
the residual learning principle is generic, and we expect that
it is applicable in other vision and non-vision problems.
2. Related Work
Residual Representations. In image recognition, VLAD
[18] is a representation that encodes by the residual vectors
with respect to a dictionary, and Fisher Vector [30] can be
formulated as a probabilistic version [18] of VLAD. Both
of them are powerful shallow representations for image re-
trieval and classiﬁcation [4, 48]. For vector quantizat']
response:
The provided context mentions that instead of trying to fit a desired underlying mapping directly, the authors introduce a deep residual learning framework that allows each stacked nonlinear layer to match another mapping explicitly. The formulated mapping is denoted as F(x) = H(x) + ... + Identity mapping, where the weights of multiple nonlinear layers are zeroed down to obtain a reference identity mapping. The authors show that if the optimal function (i.e., an identity mapping) is closer to the residual mapping than to the zero mapping, then the learned residual representations may be easier to optimize compared to learning with a new function. This is demonstrated by experiments in which the authors use a VLAD-like representation and Fisher Vector representation as shallow image representations.

📄 Source: ResNet.pdf
reference: F(x) := H(x) - x, where H(x) is the desired underlying mapping.

entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to

Abstract
The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or
convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. The best
performing models also connect the encoder and decoder through an attention
mechanism. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer,
']
response:
The proposed new simple network architecture in the paper is called the Transformer, which dispenses with recurrence and convolutional operations entirely and instead uses an attention mechanism to encode and decode information. The model achieves superior quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train than existing sequence-to-sequence models.

📄 Source: ResNet.pdf, transformer.pdf

Encoder: The encoder is composed of a stack of N= 6 identical layers. Each layer has two

To answer your question about the mechanism exclusively used for positional encoding in the Transformer, we refer to the "positional encoding" function used by the Transformer's encoder and decoder stacks. This function adds a single scalar value to each input embedding vector at the bottom of each encode and decode stack, which is multiplied by that same scalar at the top of the decoder stack. The positional encoding function serves as an initializer for the input embeddings, ensuring that all positions are attended to only once. This mechanism enables the Transformer to attend over a single position in a sequence, while also taking into account subsequent positions through the use of self-attention.

Instead of having multiple layers for self-attenion, we use a single self-attenition layer and a separate point-wise feed-forward layer to handle both encoder and decode layers. This allows us to keep the structure of the encoded/decoded sentence while still performing better than previous self-attention models in many tasks.
